SCENE-CONDITIONED MARITIME PoL — 1 · retrospective anomalies · 2 · live threat assessment · 3 · multi-hour behaviors · 4 · play-forward behaviors
Scene-Conditioned Maritime Pattern-of-Life · Cold-start (never trained on this region)

What's still anomalous — and why the scene says so

The place-conditioned model flags motion that is improbable for the kind of place it happens in. After clearing the dredged-cut chart artifacts and suppressing get-underway / anchoring transitions (a stop→cruise ramp the small model can't reconstruct), genuine anomalies remain in Hampton Roads. Each below carries its own reasoning: what the vessel did, what the model expects there, and the counterfactual—the place where this motion would be normal. That last line is the scene-understanding win: the anomaly is localized to the place, not just the track.

The map

Corrected place-token backdrop; dots are the surviving anomalies (size = confidence). Hover a dot; click to pin its card. Magenta = anomalous under every place; red = place-explained.

A few clear wins

Highest surprise-for-its-place first. Filter by place-type. Click a card to watch the maneuver animate.

How to read a card. Observed is what the vessel actually did over a ~12-minute span. Expected here is p(motion | place)—the model's learned prior for that place-type, extracted with zero hand-coding. The counterfactual re-scores the same motion under every other place-token and reports the one that would make it normal; a large drop means the surprise is genuinely about where it happened. Confidence is a conformal exceedance over the per-place 5%-false-alarm threshold.

Three false-positive classes removed to get here. (1) The chart overlay cleared 54 dredged-channel artifacts (routine tugs/pilots mislabeled by 200 m bathymetry; toggle “show cleared” above). (2) A transition guard suppressed get-underway / anchoring spans — a stop→cruise ramp the 2-layer model reconstructs poorly, flagging it “anomalous everywhere.” (3) off-raster spans where the vessel is outside the bathymetry tile, so there is no place to condition on. Everything below is a real behavior (maneuvering, loitering, speeding) that is genuinely improbable for its place — verified per-window with enc_diagnose.py. Real cures: velocity-derivative / activity-state awareness, wider raster coverage, and a dedicated learned charted-channel token (Route B).

The story — what we did, terse and complete

Thesis. Behavioral normalcy is conditional on place, and place can be encoded from static, global, chart-like geography instead of accumulated traffic history — answering a Navy SBIR's twin constraints (no onboard historical database; cold-start in never-visited regions) with explainability native to the architecture.

1 · Place vocabulary from geometry alone. GMRT bathymetry → 6 per-cell geometry features → one shared k-means codebook (K=8) across three ports. Learned with zero AIS input, yet its tokens sort vessel speed monotonically (harbor 90% stopped → deep open water 61% cruising).

2 · Scene-conditioned trajectory model. A TrajBERT-style 2-layer masked transformer: motion tokens (8 speed × 7 turn = 56-word vocabulary) + a place token per 60 s step; trained with 12-step span masking on Puget Sound + SF Bay. Hampton Roads was never trained on — every number here is cold-start. Anomaly = masked-span NLL; per-place conformal thresholds (5% FAR) calibrated on the training ports.

3 · Charts fix what bathymetry can't. Raw scoring flagged 211 spans. At 200 m resolution, dredged channels through shallows tokenize as “harbor edge,” so routine tugs flagged. Rasterizing 45 USACE National Channel Framework polygons onto the token grid re-tagged 3,809 cells — model unchanged — removing 54 false alarms (+3 new).

4 · Adversarial audit of every survivor. Per-flag diagnostics (enc_audit / enc_diagnose) bucketed the rest and confirmed two more artifact classes: get-underway/anchoring ramps the small model can't reconstruct (−45) and off-raster spans with no place to condition on (−8). 107 genuine anomalies remain, all verified as real motion (step-distance ≈ speed-implied), spanning sharp turns, weaving/loitering, and speeding.

5 · Faithful explanations. Each card reports the model's contextual expectation (what the score was actually computed against — caught when a “norm here: stopped” label misdescribed a speed-jump flag), a plain-language motion description, and the counterfactual place where the motion would be normal, with Δ nats separating place-driven from motion-driven surprise. Click any card: the real trajectory animates over ~20 km of tokenized scene.

Was this TrajBERT?

In spirit yes, literally no. The scorer is our own TrajBERT-style model — a 2-layer, d=64 BERT encoder doing masked-token reconstruction over discretized trajectories — not the published TrajBERT (nor TrAISformer, which we cite as the tokenization precedent). Two departures matter: (i) a second input stream adds a geometry-derived place token at every timestep, which is the thesis being tested; (ii) training uses contiguous span masking — with random single-token masking, scene information was provably redundant (interpolation solves it), a kept negative result. The network was trained once and stayed frozen through this entire build: anomaly scores are its masked-span NLLs; “expected here” is its softmax under the mask; counterfactuals swap the place token and re-score; confidence is a conformal quantile of its scores. The chart integration never touched a weight — it edited the model's scene inputs, which is precisely the operational argument for scene-conditioned design.
MarineCadastre AIS (Jun 2024) · GMRT bathymetry · USACE National Channel Framework · span-mask TrajBERT-style scorer (scene-aware arm, frozen) · CPU-only · public data. Trained on Puget Sound + SF Bay; Hampton Roads held out entirely.
